mej magraramble [REDACTED_NAME] ineexpect [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME] but whatever for context, i went to [NCR] for shs (you can spout your "may burgis [REDACTED_NAME] up" comments but (1) you don't know my family's financial capacity and (2) that is so aside the point im trying to make) and as some of you might know, [NCR] gives its students a 1 week break or ILW as its called the point of ILW was to give students a chance to catch up on school work AND rest from the past 2 months of the term. it even allows some to start preparing for final exams or outputs early. it typically falls on the 9th week of the term, coinciding with the UNDAS break. obviously i cant compare [NCR] and [NCR] but i would like to say that it's kind of unreasonable to expect students to attend school and complete schoolwork every week for 3-4 months. some professors might think that asking for a "wellness break" is because students are looking to slack off and not attend school. i have some personal claims and experiences against this argument. (1) if you think that a week off would "disrupt the academic calendar," it definitely does especially if the week off wasn't planned. however, this does say a lot about where the school's priorities lie. the students are well aware that a week off would disrupt academic activities—exams and presentations initially scheduled for that week get moved onto the neck where it overlaps with activities set for that next week. in short, the workload gets tougher. if you think this disrupts the academic calendar, how do you think it disrupts the plans the students have initially made to comply with their deadlines? i think it goes to emphasize how much the students NEED the break even if it risks the workload getting worse. and what is the value held by academic calendar as opposed to the well-being of students? (2) the idea of "rest when you can" is outdated to Millenials and Boomers and Gen Z doesn't like to propagate this mindset (they like to refer to this generation as a collective so I will too). scheduling your rest around your responsibilities is not efficient and neither is it reasonable. if anything, the tasks you need to complete should be scheduled around the expected 8 hours of quality nighttime sleep and 3-4 allotted meals in a day. even then, you'd need to estimate a couple more hours to rest from commuting home after school, relaxing after classes, and taking breaks between work. it's not rational to expect students—human beings btw—to work around the clock. and yet, despite scientific knowledge that "rest is productive," many students still pull late nights to keep up with their responsibilities. (3) "you have the weekend to rest" the two days between the 5 days of school? that, depending on the workload of a student, is still going to be used for schoolwork and other responsibilities? the arguments i hear about students asking for a wellness break seem to come from people who have forgotten what being a student was like. where's the empathy? just because you "toughed it out" doesn't mean we have to (again, an outdated mindset) (4) "[REDACTED_NAME] break para maglaro at gumala imbes na gumawa ng assignment [REDACTED_NAME] i-cracram din nila" valid, [REDACTED_NAME]. pero hindi ba [REDACTED_NAME] going out with friends? [REDACTED_NAME] ba [REDACTED_NAME] to travel and spend time with family? [REDACTED_NAME] ba [REDACTED_NAME], magbasa, magsulat, gumuhit, maginuman? to do anything other than the usual tasking responsibilities is to rest, is it not? the wellness break is not students asking for less workload but rather time to complete the workload in our own time while giving us time to also prioritize our well-being on top of it all. halata naman hindi madaling maging [REDACTED_NAME]—lalo na't up. [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME]. [REDACTED_NAME] at mas mahirap pa sa totoong mundo. pero hindi ba may responsibilidad [REDACTED_NAME] unibersidad to ease the weight in some way? oo, adults [REDACTED_NAME], [REDACTED_NAME] hinihingi na tratuhin kami parang bata. ang hinihingi namin ay tratuhin kami parang tao. but ok no wellness break means back to finals prep and essays [REDACTED_NAME] for the day 🏃‍♀️ [REDACTED_NAME]

(There might be grammatical errors sorry about that) (TW: suisiyde ideation/implication, but I'm not mentioning it directly as guidelines and i don't wanna flag this page just because of rambling) I really really REALLY love my bf but i don't know if i can keep going any longer, I'm trying, i really am, but nothing i used to anticipate before could keep me around anymore, not even the games, series and manga i used to enjoy seems interesting enough for me now. I don't want to burden him with anything, nor for him to blame himself if I finally have enough courage to do it. He's one of the only few people I have who actually listens to me and tries to understand me, and don't get me started on having my family as my support system as all they do is dismiss me, or tell me "pasalamat ka [REDACTED_NAME] problema mo" (you should be grateful that's the only problem you're dealing with for my non Tagalog speaking baddies) or "other people have it way worse than you" so no, they're not an option for this.. anywayyy, back to what i was yapping about, I just.. Don't know when to time it I guess, I don't want to time it on finals, i don't want him to lose focus on that and after finals is the holidays and I don't want to ruin that for him as well, but i also don't know if i can hold on for that long, it's getting worse each day, this week especially, I've been skipping school, just crying for hours nonstop then sleep, then back to crying again— i can't bring myself to get out of bed to go to school even if my parents nag and shout at me for being useless or a burden, that I'm ruining my future, wasting opportunities— to which i am, no doubt. But what is there to ruin if i don't plan on living for that long, I can't be bothered with academics right now if all I'm trying to is holding on to stay until the next Friday (unironically it's Friday right now as I'm typing this, so yay i guess) this family is just giving me more reasons to do it earlier honestly, but I'm really trying not to, I want to try to atleast spend my first and last Christmas with my bf even if I'm not really religious, all I want is for him to be happy, and yeah I'm probably doing contradictory actions, intentions, whatever. But I've made up my mind, and he did say all he wanted was for me to be happy, and i think this might do it, but it'll probably hurt him.. But I'm tired, maybe I'm just selfish, dunno.. Ok anyway thank you for reading my ramblings oktybyeee! Love you 🩷🩷

One random evening that we exchanged contacts turned into days that you made me happy. Those little good mornings and good nights, the photos we send each other about our day, the movie recommendations we exchanged, and your words that made my heart skip a beat; they truly are a cherished memory. You know I don't like horror movies, yet you recommend them and somehow it really made me less scared to watch them lol. You always listen to what I have to say. We talk for hours, even late at night without getting bored of whatever topic we thought of. You always ask how's my day, you told me you like talking to me. I also ask you about what you did that day and say good luck with your agenda as you were busier than me that time. We were fine. Everything's going well. Maybe we had a chance— or so I thought. During my birthday, you told me good morning, I replied 'have a nice day!' but you didn't greet me. I thought you would remember because that day was also the Philippine Independence Day. Easy to remember, right? But I thought that would be the worst to occur that day— you not greeting me. But the worse thing happened— you ghosted me. I waited until 11:59PM of [REDACTED_NAME] 12, holding onto that hope that you will greet me last minute. Then a week passed, I waited. A month passed, I still waited. Yet you never replied. That 'have a nice day!' from me was the last message. What went wrong? Did I do something that would make you lose interest from what we had? Or did you meet someone better than me? Someone [REDACTED_NAME] sa'yo, someone not too desperate to talk to you? What did I do? What happened? And well, maybe I was wrong too for deactivating the only account we talk to. But I want to protect my pride. When it felt like you were replying just because you're nice and you don't want to hurt me by telling me we should stop this. I don't want to look more desperate. I miss you. It's been months but I still miss you. I want to talk to you about how my day went. I want to ask you what you did earlier. I want to know more about you, but it ended just like that on my birthday. As an introvert that barely talks to anyone, your simple messages make my day. I feel so happy when I see you on my notifs. Now, I am haunted by your missing presence. I don't even know if you still remember me. And maybe you found someone better, that's why you chose to not entertain me anymore. I don't know. I need closure, but I don't know if I can still contact you. [REDACTED_NAME] sad sha. Whatever it is, I hope you're happy, [REDACTED_NAME]. Thank you for those days that you made me felt seen. Be happy. Good bye. — [REDACTED_NAME]

Ang mga Jews sa Jerusalem were living under Roman rule — nagbabayad sila ng heavy taxes, tinitiis nila ang mga harsh treatment, and waiting for their promised Messiah to set them free. The Roman Crucifixion was the most brutal and humiliating form of execution ever created. Second is Carrying the Cross. Third is Nailing to the Cross — Unlike the artistic depictions that show nails in the hands, [REDACTED_NAME], Romans often nailed through the wrists, [REDACTED_NAME] at sa paa — places full of nerves para makacause ng unimaginable pain. Every lash na kanyang nareceived, [REDACTED_NAME], every time na he gasp for air on that cross was a declaration of His undying love for humanity, for us, for you. Each page in my notebook holds a piece of my life. Some are written with care, some are messy, and a few carry the quiet stain of tears. Some pages are still blank—not because nothing happened, but because I’m still gathering the courage to write them. Inside are stories I return to often—moments that made me laugh, broke my heart, or left me angry. Some pages are heavy. They carry the weight of choices I wish I’d made differently, words I can’t take back, and silences that lasted too long. But even those pages matter. They remind me I’ve lived and I’ve learned. Sometimes I flip back just to see how far I’ve come. Other times, I close the notebook and rest, knowing that even unwritten pages are part of the story. There are still so many pages left. Each one is a chance to begin again, to stumble, and to rise. I don’t know how many more I’ll get, but as long as there’s space, I’ll keep writing. My notebook isn’t perfect. It’s stained with coffee, its corners are bent, and some pages are torn by time. But it’s mine. It’s my story. And for as long as I can, I’ll keep filling it. Life resembles a notebook: some pages can’t be rewritten, but the ones ahead are still open—to fresh ink, a steadier heart, and words shaped by experience. 💚

the three reviewing techniques (imbes na mag-aral na lang 'yan sha) rigorous - learning every bit of information that exists about a certain topic - high reward kapag talagang naisapuso mo lahat - it would take a rare kind of dedication and a whole lot of time - probably more useful in the post-grad scene efficient - reviewing based on samplexes, mock exams, and exercises - you might not completely understand how things turned out to be, but as long as you're getting the correct answers it's fine, you can figure it out in the process - probably the best and useful technique especially when aiming for good scores, [REDACTED_NAME] samplex-based. though it's not always the case [REDACTED_NAME] LAYO NG SAMPLEXES SA EXAM OR SOBRANG HINIRAPAN NAMAN COMPARED LAST YEAR (definitely not looking at you EEE 131 LE 2) lazy (but godlike) - reviewing only the key concepts. the technique is deriving from those foundational ideas. - i aspire to be someone who can pull this off - you must be EXTREMELY intelligent kung ganito ka mag-review - sana all

To provide another perspective to the previous submission on things they learned after studying in UP; You definitely don't need to go to UP (grad school here sucks) or abroad to get your graduate studies, and there are many local universities that offer better programs and scholarships for graduate studies, and even law. I agree that just having a masters degree isnt going to get you a position or good salary off the bat. Sure, having graduate studies may be an advantage for some people when applying for jobs, but being able to "sell" yourself well during interviews and applications (I mean, having a strong resume and you're able to highlight your strengths according to the job description during interviews) is a surefire way of getting that job. But please, do not expect that you're getting a high salary from the get go especially when you're a fresh grad without experience, and experience in your orgs are not "experience" in corporate. As someone who didn't join orgs or "socialized" in college, I learned to always to upskill, learn new things, and deliver my strengths to get the jobs I wanted. I never had to rely on "connections" to get good jobs, because at the end of the day, a company would hire you for your skills and knowledge. Especially for introverts out there, you don't need to feel pressured to join orgs or have a large group of friends or socialize so much during your college days just to have a backup for when you're looking for jobs. Apply for freelance or part-time jobs, while you're in college, that are related to your degree so you can have actual working experience that you can put on your resume. Also, as someone who has been working for a long time now, don't be shocked that there are HRs that still have biases towards their alumni and some have been discriminatory towards UP graduates due to the image of activism. This is why you can often find UST, [REDACTED_NAME], [NCR], and other UAAP/NCAA graduates being hired and UP graduates are rare to find, especially in large corporations. I've had HRs ask me before if I've been participating in protests or joined activist organizations, and some will turn you down once they find out you participated in these activities. And, this goes for all universities, not just in UP. Honestly, it really does come down to your skills, experiences, and how you negotiate and leverage your background. This is why sometimes in a batch, one would start with 20-25k salary, but another would start at 40k. Its about how you present yourself to companies, whether you're a fresh grad or someone with months or years of experience, regardless of the university you graduated from.

We ran into obscurity, Of stolen glances and new expressions; Patterns that shield mistaking Familiar chains. I met Derision— As haughty as my hate for all things that walk leisurely along concrete pavements: He took me before I understood the concept of taking. Cynic, anemic, polemic and insatiably barbaric, No space for the niceties. No mercy for mediocrities. Derision takes and takes. Why, despite this incredulity I yearn for him still? Somewhere in the cobwebs of synapses A memory haunts and creeps— Cynic, anemic, polemic and insatiably barbaric. I was pining for a shadow who only wished me pain. So, Derision: Please, let me and my Peace drown inebriated with loss and longing In a body stripped of sense and civility. - Affair with a Tyrant

Probably cold take by the time of posting but it is my humble opinion that a week-long wellness break is a short-sighted and ineffective solution that will likely cause more harm than good. Why it shouldn't happen now: it is unplanned for by the faculty and would push everything by a week or more. Most, if not all courses have already plotted out a schedule and pacing for the course and this disruption will more than likely mean that students still have academic requirements around the holiday break. The other likely scenario is that deadlines would be condensed and there will be more things to finish during finals week and the weeks leading up to it. Unless your expecting profs to trim the coverage of the course, an academic break now will lead to a more stressful December. Why it shouldn't happen normally: it interrupts progression and puts an additional constraint when planning for the schedule of the course. It becomes common for the faculty to rush exams and materials before the break since having these immediately after the breaks would not be student friendly. Doing almost anything after the break isn't student-friendly either. The most that can be done is to review things covered before the break and discuss what will happen now after the break. Rushing half the course before the break and squeezing everyting else after the break does not sound fun for anybody. What sould be done: re-evaluate course syllabi or even degree programs as a whole. Especially post-K12, several courses and degree programs were re-designed to be newer, denser versions, of previous courses that aim to cover the same fundamentals but in a shorter amount of time since we are supposed to assume a higher level of competency and maturity from grade 12 graduates. It's probably true, but the potential may have been higher than what can be actually observed. Still, the bottomline is a mismatch between student capabilities and faculty expectations. Whether the capabilities are too low or the expectations are too high is debatable but the debate needs to be done and needs to be backed up by evidence. - A Wide-Eyed Young Lecturer Slowly Becoming Jaded

The often overlooked problem of being privileged: people unfairly side against you. Even when you are the one who did nothing wrong. Like somehow, you owe something to the one who wronged you. I told my friends the story of why my ex and I broke up. How our goals no longer aligned. He was struggling with his debts, and I was feeling neglected because we couldn't do the travels or go to the movies anymore, how our relationship started. My friends immediately empathized with my ex, acting as if I was the problem and that it was wonderful that he was out of the relationship. I didn't even want to share anymore that he borrowed a lot of money from me, leaving me feeling used. Somehow, I felt like they would side with him anyway. Instead, I deescalated the situation by having to recognize that I was also in a better place without my ex, because I still get to pursue my studies, engage in my hobbies, and now freely date again. And my friends were happy for me, that I was privileged but self-aware, and genuinely wished me luck on my goals. But I am frustrated about what happened. I do not like that I have to somehow watch my tongue when I am going through something myself. I know I have more money, but my feelings of loneliness and inadequacy are just the same. I genuinely wish my ex well. I genuinely wish that he finds the success and happiness he continues to work really hard to attain. And I wish myself that success too, that I am also working really hard for, because even though I know I am better set up to achieve my goals, I still feel the uncertainty in every step of the way. And my friends might never empathize with that.

The importance of dating someone less fortunate than you, as a privileged isko I've recently gotten out of a relationship with someone who struggled a lot to get to where he is in life. As a sheltered [REDACTED_NAME] who drives a car to UP every day, you just don't grow up with the perspective that gets the hardworking to where they are. I always thought it was magic how some go from rags to riches. Dating him instilled a new mindset in me that I will take as I move along my life and career. We iskos probably have privileges we don't appreciate. Some of us may have connections and jobs lined up for multinational firms, or perhaps we even have families willing to sponsor us to move abroad, but let's not forget the grit that is necessary to make these opportunities possible for us. Let's not forget the hard work our parents put into setting us up well for our futures. So thanks E. Thanks for choosing to love this sheltered and lazy bum, and perhaps transforming that into future potential. I wish you the best in your studies and in your future career. I hope you wish the same for me.

In relation to the post before about a singing group in [NCR]: I never thought this day would come. It’s been more than a decade pero natrigger pa rin ako ng post na ‘to. I chose to be silent because I preferred to move on with my life, but now I’m realizing my silence may have allowed the same kind of harm to continue happening to others who simply wanted to enjoy their passions. I’m 100% sure I’m not the only one. To this day, there are only three people who know what really happened: myself (m), my husband, and a co-survivor (m) I got in touch with many years ago. Even people close to me back then never knew the real reason why I left. They just assumed I lost interest. The saddest part was that the people I thought were my friends within that group never reached out after I stopped showing up. I’m not blaming them — I completely understand the kind of loyalty that person demanded from everyone. I was under that same influence for a long time (kaya [REDACTED_NAME] i-post ‘to). It’s a complicated feeling when someone who once showed you kindness suddenly crosses a line you never wanted to cross.

Hot take: Communism doesn't work, but neither does our current hypercapitalist state today. Let's face it: no amount of idealism will change the fact that people tend to focus on self-interest first. But no amount of THAT would also change the fact that the current state of society today is in any way acceptable. So, instead of trying to fit a square peg in a round hole, why don't we just work on solutions that allow the distribution of resources to society while ensuring the individual's rights to private property and a regulated, yet relatively free, market? We live in an age of wondrous advances that can help people's lives, and yet we still bicker like children about the "best ideology" while ignoring the flaws that each and every one of them has. Why can't we make something better? Why must we be stuck with concepts literally rooted in ideas made centuries ago? -An Isko who is really irritated at the absolute state of ideological discussion rn

In connection with . Note: I used AI to mask my writing style and identity. First, I would like to acknowledge that there are genuinely kind individuals within the organization. Many members, alumni, and trainees are truly good people. Second, speaking only from personal experience as a woman, I was never subjected to any form of sexual or physical abuse committed. However, I cannot speak for others. Based on what I observed, I would not characterize the organization’s application process as involving hazing, at least not in the traditional sense. Third, the primary issue with the conductor lies in his rigid and idealistic perspective of what a choir should be. He believes that vocal harmony and synchronization require uniformity of personal values and outlook in life—an approach that mirrors how his church operates. This is where problems arise. He expects to be consulted on personal matters that should reasonably fall within individual discretion/personal life. For example, he wants to be informed if someone is dating or in a relationship. If two members date or are in a relationship, they must seek his approval. He expresses disappointment if one spends time with people he personally deems as “bad influences.” Even social drinking is discouraged, especially if he becomes aware of it. Since you all are family and he acts as your father, he must know what goes on with your lives. [Just imagine if your real parents already approved of the relationship then he disapproves of it.] Essentially, he maintains that a person’s vocal performance is somehow reflective of their personal life. To be honest, it is difficult to separate personal and professional life with this group. Fourth, the audition process is highly subjective. The “for interview” status in audition results, for instance, often indicates that although the applicant has passed musically, there are aspects of their personality that the conductor finds questionable—whether it be perceived assertiveness or arrogance (which is HIGHLY SUBJECTIVE). The more compliant and obedient one appears, the better. In my case, the interview took place at his home near [REDACTED_NAME]. Unlike other organizations that have clear requirements, his screening process revolves mainly around (1) singing ability and (2) attitude—specifically, the perceived potential to be defiant or problematic. I believe that he seems to have repeatedly misjudged individuals based on these subjective standards, making some trainees feel visibly disfavored. And because he plays favorites within trainees, some trainees will go an extra mile to earn his favor (sumisipsip, doing chores or errands for him at home, visiting him at home to bond with him) Fifth, raising his voice or shouting during rehearsals does happen. When he becomes extremely angry, he can be very vocal and intense. However, this is not entirely unusual in the choral or performing arts scene, especially when a performance or concert is near. Still, his temper can be quite severe. Sixth, it is true that some members reside with him. The trade-off, however, is that they are often expected to accompany him to various engagements or run personal errands. This arrangement depends largely on one’s willingness to accommodate such demands. Seventh, the conductor shows a lack of gender sensitivity, which is concerning for someone leading a UP-based organization. While there are LGBT members, he has made remarks such as leaving their “choices” to God or referring to being LGBT as “wrong” according to his beliefs. For a UP organization, such views are outdated and problematic. See this is why it is better to have obedient members. They rarely raise their opposition to these politically incorrect remarks. Overall, I wish the choir and its members well. It remains a talented and passionate group, but I sincerely hope it evolves with the times. The conductor’s outdated beliefs need to be re-examined, especially if he aims to nurture the next generations of choral singers.

hey i miss you. there’s not a day when i havent thought about you. well, i dont hate it nor do i like it. it’s just really youre all i could think about. i miss you, that’s it. it honestly feels like a tooth ive had extracted that my tongue keeps checking. so many things i want to talk to you about and things i want to talk to you with. i wanna know what you think abt this or that. how youre doing now. i keep thinking about you. maybe more than i could notice. you’re just really everywhere hahahaha and its okay bcs hello we’ve been together through a lot of things. i dont hate that you’re everywhere, you have been a big part of me, my growth, my life, so it’s natural that you still take up space even though we ended our relationship alr. we tried our best to be good to each other as well so there’s no hatred really just understanding and grace for each other bcs we’ve always been like that. so your space in my life will remain as it is. i treasure what we had and even what we currently (dont) have. i feel at ease that we have once experienced love in the arms of each other. youre my first love. and i will always thank the universe that it is you that i have given my youth to. i don’t think i could’ve experienced it any better if it wasn’t you. even right now that we’re apart i feel like there’s some level of intimacy in respecting that our relationship is over yet still wanting and wishing each other the best in the future. i dont wish for anything really. at the moment, i dont mind that i keep thinking about you or remembering you, its not heavy, its just a thought. i occasionally still feel sad and cry briefly abt it sometimes but it’s all good. i can manage, i will manage and get by. i dont know if this habit will ever change and if i can actively do anything abt it later on. organically maybe i will. but thats not really my focus right now. i feel a lot of security in myself that i dont feel the need to get rid of you or thoughts of you. im not really suffering at the thought of you eh. you’re just there, like you usually are. the difference is i don’t need you to be physically present with me anymore nor do i crave for your presence. i miss you and thats it. idk what the point of this was. i just noticed i thought about you and i miss you and wanted to write abt it i guess. i still love you, but we’re really better off without each other.

Participating in activism requires a certain level of privilege. How else can one so fearlessly risk their lives and their education if they didn’t have good parents or a good enough financial status to back them up in case things go wrong? For some people, education is the only way they can get out of poverty and get better lives. It’s the most secure path to take that can guarantee some change in their future. Activism is too risky.

I just want to let this out because it’s been bothering me for days, and I honestly don’t know what to do anymore. I met this guy last May. We’ve been seeing each other for a few months now — going on dates, watching movies, eating out, spending time together. He calls me baby, hugs me a lot, kisses me, and we talk almost every day. It really felt mutual, like we were building something real. He even messaged my mom once asking if we could go out, so I thought he was serious. But lately, something feels off. My mom invited him to her birthday this Saturday, and he said he couldn’t come because they were going to their province for a two-week family reunion and that there’s no signal there. Then I happened to check his mom’s [REDACTED_NAME] account — they’re clearly still in their house, not in the province. That’s when my doubts started growing. I also remembered his other Facebook account where he used to have an “in a relationship” status with a girl. He deleted the status months ago, but it’s still the same girl in his current profile picture. Recently, I even saw their photos together from a trip last January. So at this point, I’m sure they’re still together. What hurts most is that I’ve asked him several times if he had a girlfriend, and he always said no. I really liked him — I cared deeply, and I thought what we had was genuine. But now I feel like I was just the side chick all along. I don’t know how to handle this. Should I confront him? Should I tell him what I found out? Or should I just quietly walk away? Part of me wants to hear the truth, but another part of me is scared that it’ll only hurt more.

Studying in UP has been one of the most eye-opening experiences of my life — but it hasn’t been easy, especially when you hold beliefs that don’t always align with the mainstream views around you. Being a believer in President [REDACTED_NAME]’s leadership and principles in an environment where many openly criticize him often made me feel like I was walking on thin ice. I’ve had to listen more, choose my words carefully, and sometimes keep my opinions to myself just to avoid unnecessary arguments. There were days when I questioned whether my values truly belonged here — but I realized that UP, after all, is about critical thinking, not conformity. Through it all, I learned to stand firm with respect. I may have different views, but I’ve come to understand why others think differently too. That balance — conviction with openness — became one of the greatest lessons this university has taught me. Now, as I look forward to graduating soon, I’m eager to bring what I’ve learned into the workplace: discipline, decisiveness, and genuine concern for others — the very principles I admired in Duterte’s leadership. My goal is not to argue about politics, but to apply those values in action — to lead with strength, serve with humility, and get things done for the greater good.

They met long before they ever dreamed of entering [REDACTED_NAME]. [REDACTED_NAME] and [REDACTED_NAME] were childhood friends who did everything together. They walked home after school and promised that no matter where life took them, they would always be together. When they both passed the UPCAT, they believed it was fate. But fate had other plans. By their second year, they barely talked. Sometimes, they crossed paths near Palma Hall or the [NCR]. They would see each other but act like strangers. It hurt more than any fight could. “[REDACTED_NAME], you’re paired with [REDACTED_NAME] for the presentation,” their professor said. [REDACTED_NAME] froze in his seat. He hadn’t spoken to [REDACTED_NAME] properly since that one night during freshman year when they fought under the rain near Area 2. They both said words they never really meant, but neither had the courage to fix it after. His classmate asked, “You okay with that?” [REDACTED_NAME] forced a small smile. “Yeah, sure.” But he wasn’t. Not at all. When he finally saw [REDACTED_NAME] again at the library, it felt like a punch to the chest. He still looked the same. But there was a wall now, one that [REDACTED_NAME] couldn’t break no matter how much he wanted to. “Let’s just finish this project quickly,” Wonwoo said without looking at him. [REDACTED_NAME] nodded. “Okay.” His voice came out small. This was the same person who once promised him, You’ll never lose me, [REDACTED_NAME]. They worked together in silence every night. [REDACTED_NAME] wanted to talk. He wanted to say sorry. But every time he opened his mouth, he couldn’t find the right words. Until one night, he finally asked, “Why are you treating me like this?” Wonwoo didn’t look up. “Like what?” “Like I don’t exist.” Wonwoo closed his laptop slowly. “You stopped existing for me a long time ago.” [REDACTED_NAME] laughed quietly, but his voice cracked. “You don’t mean that.” Wonwoo finally met his eyes. “Where were you when I needed you? You left.” “You told me to go,” [REDACTED_NAME] whispered. “I didn’t think you actually would,” [REDACTED_NAME] said softly. “That’s what hurts the most.” They finished the project and got a perfect grade. [REDACTED_NAME] presented flawlessly, and [REDACTED_NAME] tried to act normal, pretending that nothing was wrong. But inside, his heart felt heavier than it ever had before. After class, [REDACTED_NAME] was about to leave when [REDACTED_NAME] called out, “Can we start over? Even just as friends?” Wonwoo turned around, the sunlight hitting his face. For a moment, he looked like the same boy who used to laugh with him after school. But that moment passed. “You can’t start over when one of you already moved on,” [REDACTED_NAME] said quietly, then walked away. That night, [REDACTED_NAME] found a folded piece of paper inside his sketchpad. It was [REDACTED_NAME]’s handwriting. Thank you for trying, [REDACTED_NAME]. Maybe in another lifetime, I’d still wait for you by the [NCR] benches.

I regret joining a certain special interest org. This was years ago, but what happened hurt me and made me hesitate to open up to others again. Nothing dramatic happened, but I remember one night, I went to their discord and talked about my abusive life situation, and my orgmates in the discord (this was pandemic days, I haven't seen them yet) said that I was just ruining the mood of everything all the time and that we aren't close enough for me to air my dirty laundry at them. At [NCR], life was always busy. But, there was one rivalry everyone knew — you and [REDACTED_NAME]. You were both top students. You were the type who worked hard, stayed up late, and made sure everything was perfect. Jeonghan, on the other hand, was the type who didn’t even look like he was trying but still got high grades. “Prof, I think Jeonghan's answer lacks context,” you said during a Philosophy discussion. Jeonghan smirked, leaning back in his chair. “Really? Or are you just upset because mine made more sense than yours?” The class laughed, the professor smiled awkwardly, and you glared at him. That was how it always went between you two. Everything changed when the professor paired you and Jeonghan for your thesis proposal. That was the moment you realized something strange. Jeonghan wasn’t just your rival anymore. His laugh stayed in your mind longer than you wanted. His teasing didn’t annoy you as much as before. And worst of all, you started to like him a little.

So in fixation it begins: Your baritone was enough to ensnare my attention, Which flits like a moth over odd, irreverent things. But caught me, you did— In the accumulation of moments as liquid as curiosity’s sin. I am stingy with sentiment that borders on illogic, But defeated, I succumbed. I seek you still— By the daily. By the weeks that turn to months Of yearning. In the midst of words and doctrines that entangle my synapses, I found you—cold and so alluring, so disaffected and unmoving— The perfect object to my increasing discontent. You represent All the ideations of myself in a different universe: A man only concerned with his books and his pen, And the thoughts he produces and weaves into sharp arguments. You mastered Socratic, holding it like a whip, And as you whipped me, I craved more beatings. This is the only torture I recommend. As I sat right across you in your chamber, Your short sigh—lasting a millisecond—spoke for you. A black hole. Ha! I prayed, please— Evict my presence here! Oh, the internal, insufferably loud cringefest of shame and guilt and the desire to transmute into a paper for your own crumpling! I had interrupted productive trains of thought. Unforgivable. The exchange was hardly enjoyable. I left with a profuse litany of apologies, Not only to him but to myself. And it hit me like tinnitus that rang a bell over my ivory towers. Too many cracks and crevices in my heart Gave way to this unadulterated “crush”— So, Yes, again— My apologies. I adore you so much That you belong now in a world I created to rid my suffering. Much prose has been written about you While I lie about hating you for nothing. I do not belong to you, as our worlds are entirely apart. I have sense in this regard—much sense, as the starkest madness Of a criminal convicted of crimes against inanity. To even speak of belonging is wild— As moot as imaginings That will not come to life. —LIMERENCE dickinson to otis,

To the CAL Student Council or any CAL activists, Last October 24, at around 12:40pm, at CAL atrium, you suddenly started shouting your rally chant, followed by a long speech through a megaphone. I was at the third floor during this time, already feeling under the weather from the stress of midterms and the onset of a flu, and your shouting startled me so much. I felt overstimulated, I could feel my ears ringing. I had to cover my ears as I waited for you guys to finish. I have nothing against activists and your cause, but did you have to do that speech/chant inside the college in the middle of the day, when many students are still in their classes. I don't know if you announced that activity beforehand or what, but in any case, I and many others were not informed and were completely caught off guard. Classes were also disrupted. I understand that "disruption" to the ordinary life is inevitable when fighting for change, so I admire you all who allow such disruptions. But I am unfortunately not as noble. I'm just an ordinary person from a poor family who's just trying to get through every day by slowly building my future through education. I do not have the privilege, nor the energy, to step out of the ordinary and shout along, "[REDACTED_NAME]! Tayo ay lumalaban!" [REDACTED_NAME] may pa activity kayo, [REDACTED_NAME] as a respect to the students AND professors conducting classes nearby. And please inform everyone beforehand so we can be prepared. Thank you

I feel like I’ll get canceled or a lot of hate from this, and it’s understandable, but I just really wanna let this out as someone who lives a comfortable lifestyle. I’m from UST SHS and when my family found out that I’d be taking corporate law for college, they told me that I should go to UP. At first, I thought about it—because who wouldn’t? UP isn’t just known as part of the Big Four, it’s also known for its quality in education programs and teachings. But in the end, I decided that as someone who not only goes to UST SHS without needing to worry for its tuition, and whose family is in ownership of a decent/somewhat popular hotel in our province, I know that it’s not worth it for me to go to UP when it’s a school for scholars and such. I told my family that I should just go to [NCR], UST, or San Beda, because we can afford the tuition and not have to worry. But they told me that it’s UP or nothing at all. I explained to them the whole situation on why I didn’t want to pursue UP, but they still didn’t budge and remained stubborn in their resolve that I should go to UP. Now, it’s not rare for me to rebel against their wishes or wants. I do it all the time. However, knowing my family, if they want me to choose UP, then they’ll do every means to do so. I really don’t want to give up in this, but I’m also worried because they have the choice to reject what college I choose if it isn’t UP. And I don’t really want to add more on the burgis issue in UP. I really don’t know what to do. My parents are already thinking of what review center they should enroll me for the summer for UPCAT. I’ll say this now, there’s a very high probability that I’ll end up going to UP, and I really hope I don’t get hated for it because I can’t do anything under the guise of my families expectations and wants for a subject as important as this.

“I’m busy,” I said, trying to close the door. “Okay—may I come back later?” “No. You may not. Absolutely not.” I tidied the house. Things, as I always want them to be, in their places—labeled, organized, orderly. I resisted the urge to let things arrange themselves. Nothing should be left to chance. The rules are absolute. I sighed, content. My work was done. I was at peace. Then came the knocking. “Let me in. I am a guest.” “No. You may not.” The door opened anyway. A gun, point-blank, hovered. “You may not deny me entry.” It took the living room and demanded my company. It smelled like old rain and dead butterflies; its breaths reminiscent of the fog in my attic. I listened to it weep and grumble and wail and rant. Nothing consoled it. By the time it was done with its litanies, it proceeded to wrap its frigid arms around me. "Don't leave me." It begged. "Please let me go." "Can I stay?" “I must go back to work,” I said. “I’ll return,” it answered. "I'll always be here." It left by the back door. From the window I saw it on the lawn, catatonic. I tidied the house again. — REMISSION s.l.

How did I even end up catching feelings for a guy who's so performative yet so smart it actually annoys me? And of course, he already had a girlfriend because, I’m destined to like people who are emotionally unavailable. So there I was, silently wishing they'd break up. I was like, "Maybe if they break up, I'll finally have a chance." And then it happened. THEY BROKE UP. But no, it turns out, he broke up with his girlfriend because he likes another girl. And the worst part? The girl he likes is literally my biggest pet peeve. I've been trying to flirt for days, giving him all the hints and he just didn’t notice. I found out because one of my classmates teased them right in front of me. And I was just there, forcing a smile, trying not to let my face show how much that stung. It's so pathetic. It hurts knowing that I tried so hard to catch his attention, only for him to give it to someone who's literally everything I can’t stand.

hey, anyone who feels as if they cannot belong to anything, or to anyone? a lot of people had urged me to join orgs, parties, or any volunteer groups to know someone or feel that belongingness i want. but wherever i join, no matter how much time i spent in that moment, i never feel any sort of that feeling. i'm like a spare part in anyone's group, in any org i join. i try my best to fit in, to erase parts of myself that people find disagreeable, only for me to become more invisible to them. and i've been searching, finding my place for the past 3 years in up, up to no success. even having friends is painful, because somehow, they end up having different agendas, join up other friends, or just eventually drifted away. it got to a point that whenever i am spending a good moment with someone, i would always think how long i would be spending time with them before we eventually separate. i think it's high time for me to finally accept that i am a lone wolf who will never find their place, constantly drifting towards something which is never permanent.

i wish those who defend their favorite celebrities would also just as zealously hold them accountable. there are so many celebrities who remain silent on such relevant and morally/ethically disastrous issues for the sake of being more palatable to the masses by being apolitical or fence-sitting. there are some who are even downright cruel. idolatry, i think, isn't just an issue of the religious or political kind — when you dedicate yourself to defending and standing by this person you are often blindsided by your fanatacism that you don't see that they've lost their principles, or worse, never had them in the first place. it matters how little they were impactful to popculture or how "iconic" they are. entertainment and comedy is fleeting enjoyment that you chase in pursuit of escapism from real problems that often times feel inescapable. if your ideals are so flimsy that you'd bend them for the rich and out of touch, how will you stand against real propaganda? how will you stand against politicians who promise you everything but give you nothing? pursue truth, transparency and justice in everything. don't let your beliefs degrade over fragile and parasocial connections with your favorite celebrities.

When people say, ‘The rich are arrogant and out of touch,’ they say it like it’s a moral accusation. But if someone never experienced poverty, of course they won’t feel it the same way. That’s not arrogance—that’s just the reality of a different life condition. Some people believe suffering gives them moral authority. But pain without growth is just pain. It doesn’t make you righteous—it just makes you bitter. You don’t earn the right to judge someone just because you suffered more. The same people who say ‘don’t flex wealth’ are often the ones who dream of getting out of poverty fast and would flex the moment they could. And that’s fine. Wanting to enjoy the fruits of your work, that’s human. The problem isn’t that some people got rich. The problem is when resentment replaces ambition—when hating those who escaped poverty feels easier than working to escape it yourself. Escaping poverty doesn’t make you arrogant—demanding people stay poor just to fit your narrative does. Don’t glorify pain and demonize success. Don’t drag people back into poverty, make sure more people get out.

When people say workers are exploited under capitalism, I don’t dismiss it — exploitation exists. But let’s be precise: exploitation happens most where people have no leverage. If someone has only one job option and no bargaining power, employers can dictate the terms. That’s not just cruelty — that’s an economic imbalance. So while it’s valid to critique the system, we should also recognize this: the more skill, education, network, or capital a person has, the harder it is to exploit them. A programmer with rare skills doesn’t get exploited — companies compete to pay them. So yes — the system is harsh. But in harsh systems, the best shield is competence. Instead of telling people they are doomed to be exploited forever, we should be teaching them how to build leverage so that exploitation becomes mathematically unprofitable. We shouldn’t romanticize poverty by making it sound permanent and unsolvable. In a world where you can be exploited for being replaceable, the real rebellion is becoming irreplaceable.

State Universities don’t exist as welfare centers. They exist as national human capital investments. The goal of a university is to gather the most capable minds—rich or poor—because national progress depends on productivity and innovation, not pity. If UP only accepted poor students out of sympathy and rejected high-performing rich students, that wouldn’t be justice—it would be intellectual downgrade. We don’t strengthen the poor by excluding the rich. We strengthen the poor by allowing them to compete with the rich and prove that they belong on equal ground even if they don’t have the same resources. That’s dignity. We don’t create equality by making someone step down. We do it by helping more people step up. Poverty is a disadvantage, not a disability. When we say ‘the poor can’t compete unless we remove the rich,’ we are not defending them—we are insulting them. Money can buy access to review centers, but it can’t buy discipline. UP is living proof: there ARE poor students here—not because they were pitied, but because they outworked people who had more. The presence of rich kids who could afford private school but choose to enter UP is a form of signal that UP is the top choice. That signal raises the institution’s brand, which then raises the value of the diploma of all graduates—rich or poor.

“It’s sad that the world is ruled by money.” “We’re trapped in a system where we have to work for survival — we’re slaves to capitalism.” “Living for money means our lives are meaningless.” Money is a measurement of value, time, and contribution. It’s not evil — it’s a language humans created to signal exchange. Calling it slavery dismisses the agency of people who use it to build better lives. To say ‘we are trapped in the money system’ is to romanticize a fantasy world where survival has no cost and value just appears. But survival has always required effort — from fishing, farming, hunting, trading. Money just made value exchange scalable. People didn’t start working because money exists. People invented money because we already had to work. Yes, money can corrupt. But it can also educate a child, save a life through medicine, build a school, or buy someone their first book. Money is not the enemy — corruption is. Lack of agency is. Blaming money is the easiest way to avoid responsibility. Saying ‘we’re trapped by money’ is just another way of saying ‘I want comfort without effort. Blaming the system is easier than admitting someone played it better.

I'm starting to develop feelings for my friend who's also my orgmate. To be honest, she was already my type when we were just applicants during our freshmen year, but it didn't develop into anything more than that. Perhaps there were a few times here and there when I entertained the idea of it in my mind but I never acted upon it. However, fast forward to our sophomore year, things have started to change. After spending more and more time with her in the org, I'm starting to see her more in a romantic light and not just as a friend. I'm not even sure if she remotely feels the same way towards me or if she's even the least bit interested in me. There were a few times this past month or so when I thought there might've been something but I've always just chalked it up to my delusions. I didn't want to assume anything. More importantly, I didn't want to get attached when there's nothing there. I remember one time when the org was out drinking, she said something along the lines of not wanting to date or fall in love with a friend. I find myself at a loss on what I'm supposed to do. I don't want to just keep it to myself and carry on being friends while I have these feelings. At the same time, however, I also don't want to just confess out of the blue and end up ruining the friendship we have right now. The last thing I want to do is make it awkward between me and her in the org. Hopefully, I'll figure everything out soon enough. We will be drinking again soon with the org, and I only hope I don't get too tipsy and end up spilling the beans to her.

“The Weight of Quiet Things” There are days when I wake up and everything already feels too loud. Deadlines. Expectations. People. Myself. It’s like standing in the middle of a crowded room where everyone’s talking, laughing, moving — and yet somehow, all I can hear is silence. The kind that presses against your chest and makes it hard to breathe. Maybe it’s burnout. Maybe it’s something worse. All I know is that I’m tired — not the kind of tired that sleep can fix, but the kind that lingers even after rest. I used to think I could handle everything I signed up for. Classes, org work, training — all of it. I told myself I just needed to try harder, push a little more. But lately, it feels like everything’s slipping away from me no matter how tightly I hold on. I hate it. I hate how I’ve started missing requirements, skipping classes, avoiding things I used to love. It’s like I’m watching my life move forward without me, and I’m stuck in place, unable to catch up. And then there’s the loneliness. I’m surrounded by people — friends, teammates, classmates. Some are amazing, some are just... there. But even with all of them around, I still feel alone. And that’s what frustrates me most. How can I be surrounded by so many people, yet still crave connection? How can I feel this empty when there’s so much presence? It makes me feel greedy, ungrateful even. But it’s not that I don’t have anybody. It’s that sometimes, it feels like nobody has me. [REDACTED_NAME] was supposed to be my safe place. I grew up in a religious home — prayers before meals, Sunday services, the constant reminder that God is always there. And I do believe that. I believe He listens, that He saves, that He loves. But I also feel undeserving of it. It feels unfair to ask for His grace when I’m still this messy — when my life is all over the place, and my faith feels half-formed. I know God takes people as they are, broken or whole, but something inside me keeps saying, “Not yet. You’re not ready. You’re not worthy.” And maybe that’s the hardest part — wanting to come closer but being afraid of what He might see. I realize now that I’ve been bottling things up for too long. I keep everything contained until I can’t anymore, and when it all spills out, I clean up the mess halfway before screwing the lid shut again. It’s a cycle — one I hate, but one I understand too well. Sometimes, I start crying mid-thought, and by the time I reach the middle of what I want to say, I stop feeling anything at all. It’s like my emotions run out of battery. But writing this — even if it’s just words on a screen — feels like letting air into a room that’s been closed for too long. It’s not a solution. It’s not a miracle. But it’s something. Maybe healing doesn’t come in grand gestures. Maybe it starts quietly — in the small act of being honest, in admitting that you’re not okay but still trying to understand why. Maybe faith isn’t about being ready. Maybe it’s about showing up anyway, even when you’re lost, even when you’re scared, even when you feel like you don’t deserve to. Maybe the quiet was never empty after all. Maybe it’s just been waiting for me to listen.

Well, what are Large Language Models? They are statistical pattern matchers based on the syntax of user prompt (e.g. what the user typed) and what the model knows about it and bound only by the context limit, the data trained, and the volume and quality of information indexed, and the physical scaling laws that govern the dynamics of training Large Language Models. We have come a long way from where Transformers were mere theoretical to - where it first came out for public use and in 23-24 where developers began integrating the usage of vector databases, search function via search engines, Chain of Thought, and Agentic AI (LLM to LLM communication). However, we haven't really solved the core problem of it making wrong responses and as the year passes by, competition gets stiffer and it gets more difficult to contribute to the growing body of knowledge to this research area. It's gotten to the point where my approach to contributing to this wonderful area of research in AI has numbed my mind against LLMs. It's sort of analogous to a clean slate lobotomy and that I've resorted into researching areas in the 1900s to find a solution in . Honestly, the PhD journey is a dense, foggy forest full of mysterious terrain to carelessly tread and animals waiting to pounce at you should your psyche and your willpower drain out. It's like the case that you should be treading with your lamp, one step at a time and savoring the difficulty of treading such murky ground. Just grateful that my two advisers are interested in my topic and for that, there is nothing you'd wish for these two advisers who are as passionate as you to change the world and the landscape of AI. - Gold and Silver PhD Data Science freshman

I passed the UPCAT batch . I should've been an [REDACTED_DEMONYM] ng bayan—something I've always dreamed of but when the results came out my mother died. And even though tuition was free, everything else wasn't. I needed money for my dorm rent, allowance daily living expenses and others things my father couldn't provide as a single parent and my father was old to do heavy work. I tried to convince myself that it was okay, that maybe it just wasn't meant for me, but the truth is, it still hurts. So i stayed. I enrolled in a university here in our province near our house and honestly i didn't like but everyday it's hard. The environment was toxic, the course I'm taking wasn't something i love but it was something practical I can't help but to wonder what kind of a person i would've become if life had given me a little more time, little more help. It's heartbreaking when your dreams don't die, but you have to bury them anyway because reality doesn't give you a choice. I'm still trying to accept where i am now, still trying to find meaning in this path i didn't choose. But deep down, that kid who once dreamed of being an [REDACTED_DEMONYM] ng bayan still lives in me, quietly hoping that someday, all this will still make sense. Even if you pass the hardest exam of your life, but lose the people and that means that would've made the victory complete. Still, i know my mom would've been proud not because i made it to UP but because i kept going, even when everything fell apart. ❤️‍🩹

I'm unsure if I'm experiencing burnout, but honestly, the only things keeping me here are the responsibilities and tasks I feel I can't abandon right now. Specifically, I'm mentoring a group of students I oversee, aiming to support them until they can stand on their own (&better) political organizers. I'm not a perfect student leader; I've made mistakes in events and decision-making before that have had significant impacts on our org. Though the organization allows room for growth and improvement, my past experiences seem to have left me walking on eggshells. I've realized these subtle comments have eroded my confidence, making me feel less capable.
